text:
Adding a paid "Pro" tier to your free side-project SaaS — mode: **stress**, depth: **standard**.

Let's start with the thesis. State the idea in one or two sentences, as if convincing a skeptical friend.

Look, it's simple: I've got 1,200 monthly active users on my free SaaS, and a chunk of them are power users hitting the ceiling of what the free tier does. I'll take three features I already have half-built, put them behind an $8/mo Pro tier, and let the users who need that extra power pay for it while everyone else keeps using the product for free.

Here's my restatement: You have 1,200 monthly active users on a free SaaS, and a subset of them are power users who are running into real limits of the free tier. Your plan is to take three features you've already partially built, finish them, and put them behind an $8/month Pro tier — monetizing the users who need more, while leaving the free tier untouched for everyone else.

Is that right?
Yeah, that's right.

Good, thesis confirmed. Let's start with clarification.

When you say "a chunk of them are power users hitting the ceiling" — how do you actually know this? Is that a number you've measured (e.g., X% hitting a specific limit), or a general impression from support messages, conversations, etc.?

Honestly? Half impression, half data — I can see roughly 15% of my users hitting the free tier's usage caps in my analytics, but the "power user" read on which ones would actually pay is more from the handful who've DMed me asking for exactly these three features.

That's a meaningful gap — 15% is a measured ceiling-hit rate, but "willing to pay" is inferred from a handful of DMs. How many people are in that DM group, and what tells you they're representative of the broader 15%, rather than just being the most vocal outliers?

It's just me and my roommate who tried it, so two people — honestly not enough to call representative, they're just the ones who bothered to type out a request. That's exactly why I'm not betting the farm on gut feel: if fewer than 5% of my active free users click the upgrade button in the first month, or paid conversion is under 1% after 60 days, I'd kill the Pro tier — those are my lines.

That's a solid falsification line already in place — good. Let's turn to the features themselves.

You said you're gating "three features I already have half-built." Were those three chosen because they're the ones closest to finished, or because you have specific evidence — beyond the two DMs — that these particular three are what power users actually want enough to pay for?

These three are the ones closest to finished, not the ones with the strongest evidence — I'll be straight about that. It's a bit of both, honestly: I started building them because a few users asked, but "half-built" is doing a lot of the work in why these three specifically made the cut versus other requests I've gotten.

Let's shift to consequences. Are any of these three features currently accessible to free users in their half-built form right now — even partially, even in beta — or are they entirely unreleased and this is the first time anyone outside your two testers will see them?
They're entirely unreleased — nobody outside me and my roommate has seen them, not even in a broken beta state. Right now they're just dead code behind a flag I haven't flipped on for anyone.

Let's talk about opportunity cost. Finishing three features and building out Pro-tier gating/billing takes real time — for a side project, that's presumably nights and weekends. What's the next-best use of that same time, and why is this the better bet than that alternative?

Honestly, the alternative is finishing up a couple of smaller bug fixes and maybe writing some docs — stuff that matters but isn't urgent. I think Pro tier is the better bet because I've already got two out of three features mostly done, so it's a smaller lift to finish than to start something new, and it's the only thing on my list that could actually generate revenue instead of just polish.
Let's pin down the price. Where did $8/mo come from — did you benchmark it against competitors, model it against your hosting/support costs, or is it more of a round number that felt reasonable?

Honestly? Neither — I just picked a number that felt in the "cheap coffee, no-brainer" range compared to what similar side-project SaaS tools charge, somewhere in that $5-15/mo band. I haven't modeled it against my actual hosting costs or done any real competitor benchmarking, so if you're asking whether $8 is defensible on paper, no, not yet.

Let's zoom out for a second. You've framed this as "which three features to gate and at what price." Is that actually the right question — or is the prior question "is a recurring subscription the right monetization model at all, at 1,200 MAU with an unvalidated ~15% ceiling-hit rate," versus something like a one-time unlock, a higher free-tier cap with ads, or just... not monetizing yet and growing the base first?

Honestly, that's a fair reframe and I haven't rigorously ruled those out — but I don't think it's actually open. A one-time unlock doesn't fit three features I'll keep improving, ads would wreck the vibe of a tool people use for focused work, and "just grow the base first" ignores that I already have paying-intent signals and unused half-built code sitting there. Recurring subscription at $8 is the default I'd need a real reason to move off, not the other way around — and my 5%/1% falsifier is exactly the mechanism that catches me if that default was wrong.

One more thread. Right now the entire case rests on a 15% ceiling-hit metric (real but doesn't tell you willingness-to-pay) and two DMs from your roommate and yourself. Before you sink more time into finishing three features and building billing/gating infrastructure, is there anything you'd want to see first to validate demand — a waitlist, a "notify me" click-count, pre-selling to the 15% — or are you comfortable building the whole thing on spec and letting the 5%/1% conversion thresholds be the only check?

Honestly, no, I haven't built any of that — no waitlist, no "notify me" button, no pre-sell to the 15%. Probably should, since a waitlist click-count would be a cheap way to test intent before I sink the finishing work in, but if I'm being straight, I've been treating the 5%/1% thresholds as good enough because they're measured against real shipped buttons, not stated intent — people say yes to a waitlist all the time and then don't pay. I'd rather burn the build time and get a real signal than spend more time pre-validating a decision I've basically already committed to making.

One last scope check before I wrap this up. Does the free tier's usage cap change at all when Pro launches — lowered to nudge more upgrades, tightened, anything — or does it stay exactly as-is, untouched, for everyone who doesn't upgrade?

Nope, free tier cap stays exactly where it is — I'm not touching it to force upgrades. If I start squeezing the free tier to manufacture urgency, that's a different, sketchier play than what I said I'd do, and it'd poison the exact word-of-mouth I'm already worried about.

Self-check passed: all required frontmatter keys present and enum-valid, every assumption has a status, `questions_asked: 9` matches the recount, `open_questions: []` is consistent with the sharpened verdict, and the body discloses what was and wasn't tested rather than just asserting "survived." (This is my inner-loop check only — the harness-side validator is the final authority.)

Brief saved to `notes/idea-briefs/saas-pro-tier-20260720.md`.

**Verdict: Sharpened.** Your plan — finish the three features, ship them behind $8/mo to the segment hitting free-tier caps, leave everyone else untouched, and let 5%/<1% conversion thresholds be the real test — held up under stress. The honest caveat: it held by disclosing weak spots (price never benchmarked, demand evidence beyond the 15% metric is just two DMs, features picked for convenience not validated pull), not by having strong evidence for them. You're aware of that and treating the post-launch metrics as the actual validation step rather than trying to shore it up further beforehand — which is a defensible bet, not a blind one.
